speaker: interviewer
text: This is the beginning of tape two, the interview with Morris Rubenstein. Did you want to clarify when you were born?

speaker: interviewer
text: November 15th, 1924?

speaker: interviewee
text: November 15, 1924.

speaker: interviewer
text: But according to the deportation camps, you were born in what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: '28. You know, it's OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We started to talk a little bit about some of the refugees who came across the border--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --between the Polish and the-- excuse me, the German and Russian zones. Did you know about what was going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: You mean on the German side? No, I didn't know. I didn't know at all. I-- I-- I heard what they we're talking about, but I really-- I didn't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you hear them talking about?

speaker: interviewee
text: What-- they-- they didn't-- they didn't talk about the ghettos or about the concentration camps, you know. I didn't even know what was going on at all, you know? And-- and I don't think it was-- I didn't pay attention. I just was too young, you know, for it, you know, to analyze it, or whatever, you know? So-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] but it was bad for the-- it was bad for me, for the Russians. I-- I don't even know how to explain. You know, it's hard to explain. It's hard to-- it's hard to explain it.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did things change and get worse for you and your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: You mean for the Russians?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, after the Russians.

speaker: interviewee
text: After the Russians-- you mean when the Germans came in?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the Germans, came in-- you know, the war it starts-- you know, it starts Sunday, you know. They came in, you know. They start bombing. And-- and Friday the Germans came in-- Friday morning.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1941, I think-- in 1941. It was June, 1924, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry, it was June--

speaker: interviewee
text: June-- it came in in June in 1941.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what do you remember about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- I remember, you know, they-- they-- they came in. And it was a bridge. And I-- I saw them come. And I remember when the war start, when the Russians start to go back, you know, they were-- they were staying and-- and And mother and I, you know, we went, you know, to a-- a farm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did? You and who?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we didn't know the farmer, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, no, you went where? I'm sorry, I didn't hear you.

speaker: interviewee
text: To a farmer-- we went away from the city.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who? You and who? You went with who? Your mother or with someone else?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mother. Just with my mother. And-- and I saw the Russians, you know. They were running, you know, with horses, with some trucks, and everything. And our neighbor, he asked me if I saw the Germans coming. But I did see did Germans come. But I thought maybe they were Russians. You know, I didn't see it. And after the next day, you know, the Germans were there already. And we start to go on-- go on back home already. So on the way, you know, my mother went home, and everybody was loading. You know, so I was loading too.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I-- they broke-- they broke in in a warehouse, and I got out eggs. I got apples. And I got some flour, you know, I brought it home. And then I brought home-- you know what you put on the horses where they pull the wagons-- I brought home.

speaker: interviewee
text: And after the Germans came in, they occupied. And the first Germans that-- that came in, they said, Juden, we are not going to bother you. But when the next Germans will come in, they're going to. Watch out. They're going to bother you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Someone said that to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they said it to the Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: The German soldiers said that to the Jewish people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. You know, they are not going to bother us, but the next Germans, when they will come in, you know, they'll bother us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after the front, you know, the first line went away, like, to Lutsk, you know, whatever, and I think in three or four days, the Germans, they came in, you know, with horses, artillery, with everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they took me and a neighbor boy. You know, we fed horses. I forgot what else we did when we were down there. And then a German-- another German came over. You're not allowed to employ Jewish-- Jewish boys, you know, Jewish help. So we-- so we left. I know they give us something to eat. And I got so charred burn.

speaker: interviewer
text: And after a few days, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you know, they told all the Jews to come out, you know, to the-- to the-- it was a place. It was like a [INAUDIBLE]. Magistrate, you called it. You know, everybody should-- should come-- should come out to the-- what do you call it? Where the mayor is-- what do you call this place? The city--

speaker: interviewee
text: City hall. You know, it used to be the city hall for Poland, like. So they all went out. All the-- the Jewish people from the whole city, like. And I--

speaker: interviewer
text: This is the city of Rozyszcze?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rozyszcze-- in Rozyszcze, yeah. And I went out. I went in-- I went into the-- to the wheat, and I hid myself. I hide myself. And after they-- they picked Judenrat-- Judenrat, a Jewish council, you know-- I think they took out all the young ones. They picked-- they took out a lot of them. And they took them on to a church, not far, you know. And they killed them all out. They killed all the young people, the Jewish people out. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you know that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I know that after, you know, a Polish from the church, he came over and he told-- and he told us, you know, they kill them all out. But the wives, the mothers, didn't believe it. you know, because they made-- they made them sign, like, write a letter, you know, like they went to work, whatever, you know. So-- and after, like, everyday, you know, we had to go to work, like work on the roads or-- or on the ditches. You know, they found work. And I went. You know, I was loading on the railroad, you know-- rye, corn, wheat, everything, you know. And then I had-- I-- I put on two pairs of pants. And I used to take the wheat and put it in my pocket, you know. And you-- I used to-- and I used to tie the other pants, you know. And I used to bring it home. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And my mother used to cook it, you know, and make-- that's how we survived, you know. But they didn't-- they didn't believe it. And now every day, the Ukrainian police, you know, used to go-- they used to run out the Jews, and every day they used to kill-- kill Jews. That's how they killed them out, the young ones. And I used to watch. I used hide myself every time. And one time, my cousin, he came. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he went into the shop, you know. He start working, you know. And I-- I heard cries, and I-- I went out to the shop, and I looked up, you know, like-- it was a little [INAUDIBLE] like-- like this. And I saw the Ukrainian police. They were taking out a Jewish guy, you know, like to take him away to kill him, whatever. And the two daughters were yelling, you know, let my father go. So I went in, and I told my cousin. I said, you know, they are taking the Jews and everything. He said, it's OK. He said, you go up on the attic. And he said they wouldn't bother him because he's a tradesman, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went up on the attic. And he told me, take the ladder up too. When they come in, they wouldn't know. I didn't even hear. You know, they came in. I guess they took him away.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I-- I heard my mother crying. You know, she really cried and cried. And I looked out, you know, through the-- through the boards from the attic. And I saw the rabbi, you know, my rabbi. You know, he went to the dead. And other people, they-- they-- they went to the dead. And I heard machine guns, you know, shooting everything. I was still in the attic. And after the shooting stopped, and it was quiet, I went. My mother was still crying and crying. And I went down. When she saw me, she didn't know what to do with me, you know, and everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: She didn't know you were hiding?

speaker: interviewee
text: She didn't know I was on the attic. You know, she thought took they took me away too, you know. So-- and this would happen every-- every day, you know. I had to go to work-- not just me, women and men. And I went to work one time, you know. See, like, there was holes, so we had to take dirt and put it on the holes, you know, when they drive through with wagons or with the whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was I and another boy. We went to ask for bread, you know, by the farmers. And a German on a coach with two horses-- see, he was sitting there. It's hard for me to explain, you know. He was sitting there, and he had a driver who drove him. So he saw us. And first, we had the-- the Star of David on, you know-- on our-- on the arm. And so he called us over. And he asked us where we are going. I forgot already.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he had a whip. And he started whipping us. And he told us to go back to work. You know, it was a little farm, maybe a half a block, not even half a block away. So we went-- we went back. And then the Ukrainian, he asked us, you know, where we went, you know. And he had a big stick. You know, a lot of times he used to hit us over the head, wherever. He hit us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And every day-- every day-- they killed Jewish people-- every day. And this is what was going on, you know? You didn't report, though, when they asked you to come to the city hall? You mentioned this a little while ago.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't report, you went and hid in the wheat?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't go. I hid myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't get into trouble? They didn't come looking for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, they didn't come looking for me, no. And then, you know, they picked the Judenrat. Right then, they knew the names from the Judenrat. They picked them out, and they made a council-- a Jewish-- a Jewish council. And when they wanted people to go to work, they went to the Jewish council, and they said they needed 15 or 20 Jews to go to work. You know, and they had to. Some of them, they-- they came back, and some of them were killed. You know, it depends.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the Judenrat call you to work, or you worked separately outside of the Judenrat?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, they called me to work too, you know. And I had to go to work. And this was going on, you know, til they made the ghetto. I think it took about 4, 5, 6, or 8, maybe 10 months. I forgot already. You know, it's about 50, 52 years ago. And they-- but after-- after, I think, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] before the ghetto-- you know, so my mother died. It was, I think, two weeks, maybe a month before, you know, because a lot of times, she didn't let me go to work. And even the Judenrat, you know, you know, sent [INAUDIBLE]. And she-- she went to the farmers, you know, like to get bread or whatever. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: You were supposed to work, but you didn't work. Your mother didn't let you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Didn't let me go. I was hiding. You know, I was hiding.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your mother hiding?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. She didn't. She didn't hide, you know. The only-- The only time, you know, they bothered a woman was when-- when she went, like, on the farm, you know, like, to ask for something, ask for bread or what. You know, so a lot of times, you know, they bothered them. But like this, they didn't. They didn't bother them. When there was a raid in the ghetto, they took women to work too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your Ukrainian neighbors bother you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Ukrainians?

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your relationship with the Ukrainian neighbors?

speaker: interviewee
text: You mean with the Germans were-- see, we didn't have-- we didn't have too many Ukrainian neighbors.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned Ukrainian police.

speaker: interviewee
text: The police. It was police, you know. Ukrainian-- Ukrainian police, you know, they round up all the Jews for the Germans, the Ukrainian police. They collaborated with the Germans. All the Ukrainians collaborated, you know, with the Germans. But I want to tell you after, you know, my mother got sick-- she got sick one day, and the next day, she really got sick. She shaked. And I had a cousin. His name was Schmuel, you know, Schmelik, whatever. And I called him. And she was just yelling, Moishe, Moishe, Moishe, Moishe. And he came. And we were both sitting, you know, and she died. She died, and it-- it must have been around January, because I have to say Kaddish next Saturday, you know, the 11th. The 10th or 11th, I have to say [HEBREW], you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did she die?

speaker: interviewee
text: She died-- I think she got frozen, when she used to go to get food and everything, because she didn't let me go. And she died, and she was-- we couldn't bury her, because they had to get a [INAUDIBLE] from the German, from the head German. It was-- I forgot. See, I forgot a lot of things. You know, but a commandant-- they had a commandant. He was a storm trooper, or whatever you call them. And she was laying, like, seven days, you know, on the floor til we got allowed to bury her. And-- and the Judenrat sent a guy. You know, he was sitting with me, you know every day and every night. And then, after she got buried, you know, neighbors came, you know, in and out, and some were sitting with me. And I think in a week, or two weeks, a month, you know, they rounded up all the Jewish people, and they chased them into the ghetto, you know. It was all Ukrainian. I didn't see no Germans, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was like a seven mile-- it was a bridge, like what the water used to go through. You know, they threw down one guy. I don't know if he didn't work too fast or whatever. And they threw him down in the water, I don't know how you'd survive.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in the back was a way that what they could go to right in the back of our house. So the Ukrainian-- you know, man or woman, they were running. And they were hitting them with chains, with sticks, you know. One lady, a young lady, you know, they hit with a stick, and I think it was a nail in it, you know. And the blood was coming out and everything. And they closed up-- they closed up the ghetto. And every-- every-- then I started going-- going to work, you know. I couldn't hide anymore, because the house was full. You know, our house-- our house was left in the ghetto. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- you were alone now. Your mother died.

speaker: interviewee
text: Alone, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So who were you with? What was that like to be by yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well it was a real, real bad, you know. And I used to-- when I used to go to work, I used to go out, you know, and ask the farmers for bread, you know. Some of them used to give me some bread. And then I-- I had, you know-- it was some soap I had. And I had what I took from the horses. I used to sometimes run out from the ghetto and exchange it for food with the farmers.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were living-- well, first of all, how was the ghetto set up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was the ghetto set up? One day the Judenrat announced that they were going to be a ghetto? Or how did that come about?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. They just-- they just chased in the Jewish people to the ghetto, and that's it. There was no announcement. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there boundaries? Were there walls?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there were no walls. It was just-- it was just-- It was a cemetery-- it was up to the cemetery, the ghetto. And then it was the bridge where the water went down. And our house was-- and this was the ghetto. Now, you couldn't get out from the ghetto. After, you know, they had-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] --the Star of David, a yellow Star of David on front, and down on the back, one Star of David. And when you-- the Germans could see the yellow from far away. If you ran out of the ghetto, they caught you, you were killed. And the Ukrainian, you know, the Ukrainian police could kill-- kill too.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many were living in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: In this ghetto? I wish I knew. You know, it must be-- they must have killed out 2,000 to 3,000. There must have been about 3,000, 4,000. And our house was packed. It must have been about six or seven families in our house.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were alone, but with those other--

speaker: interviewee
text: With those other people. You know, there I was alone.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any of those people?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember-- well, it was my cousin's, you know, wife and her two sisters. And my cousins, they had two little girls, but I forgot their names. You know, I know her name was Ruhu. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And you talked about sometimes you did leave the ghetto? You would run away?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not just to run away-- I used to go to work. You know, or get out of the ghetto-- if they would catch, they would kill. But I used to go out a lot of times.

speaker: interviewer
text: To work?

speaker: interviewee
text: To work.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work were you doing then, and how come you didn't get into trouble?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when-- when-- I used to go out to the ghetto, I didn't go to work. I just-- I just went out, and I used to go house to house there, and beg for food from the farmers. And I used to come back. When I used to come back, some Jewish people, you know, they used to walk back. I walked with them back, you know. And a lot of times, when some of them walked to work, I walked to work with them. But I used to run-- run away from them, you know, and go and ask for bread, or whatever. But most of the time, I used to go to work with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you-- what sort of work were you doing then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like loading the railroad cars, you know, or unload coal from the-- from the railroad cars, or other stuff.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you did this for whom?

speaker: interviewee
text: For the Germans, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who was giving you food?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans didn't give food.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said you-- how did you get food? You said you went out to the farmers?

speaker: interviewee
text: To the farmers, you know, and sometimes, even when I worked for the Germans, I used to run away, you know, because there was a lot of us. There was about, 15, 20, 30, you know. And I used to ask the farmers, you know, for food, whatever. And one time--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where they non-Jewish, the farmers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Non-Jewish. No, there was no Jewish farmers. There was no Jewish farmers.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were Polish or Ukrainian?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some Ukrainian, some Polish, You know, most of them, you know, were Ukrainians, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry, I interrupted you. And one time-- what happened one time when you went out?

speaker: interviewee
text: One time, the-- the Judenrat, you know, they took us to work. And on the-- on the ice-- you know, it was like the Canadian-- between Detroit and Windsor. It really froze. And we had to make holes in the ice, you know. So I had an ax. And we had a saw. You know, we had to saw the-- the ice. It was two Germans for a while, watching us. And one German-- he was a-- a real Gestapo. They used to call him the [? Brazilop ?] Commandant. See, what they used to take away-- they took away from the Jewish people the cows, the goats, the sheep, everything. Everything, they took away. And he-- he took care on it, you know, this commandant. He used to kill a Jewish person. And he used to come into the ghetto, and he said he just killed a dog, you know. So-- and he was there while we were working on the ice. And he-- and he took us all, and we had to lift up a tank-- a Russian tank, you know. He examined it-- you know, the machines, or the motors, and everything. And we were-- we were afraid, you know, he might shoot us, so. But he didn't. The bullets, you know, were like gold. You know, and the gun was right [INAUDIBLE], you are a tall guy. And after, the Ukrainian killed him. And when they killed him, he-- he-- the Gestapo came right away. And before he died, he said, a Jewish person didn't kill him, because they would kill all the Jews. They killed him because he took the cattle from them, he took the horses, he took everything from them. But anyway, so then we didn't have nothing to eat. We came back, you know to-- so we asked a German if we could go and buy some bread. One-- so he said, OK. One guy went, and while he brought the bread, another German came, a Gestapo. So they had to put the bread under the snow, you know, to-- to hide it. And one boy, while we were taken-- I remember his name was Bill-- he almost-- he went in, and I caught him.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is the end the tape two. And we'll pick up with this story with Morris Rubenstein.
